title_en: Commercial and Family Law: Cross-School Analysis
title_ar: قانون التجارة والأسرة: تحليل مقارن بين المذاهب
content_en:
The commercial and family law sections of Imam Muhammad's recension provide a systematic cross-school analysis of the legal traditions in the Muwatta. The commercial sections are particularly important for understanding the early development of Islamic commercial law, since they preserve the Maliki traditions on commercial transactions alongside the Hanafi positions, creating a documentary record of the earliest systematic dialogue between the two schools on these matters. Imam Muhammad's presentations of the Hanafi positions on sales, contracts, partnerships, and prohibited transactions are valuable primary sources for the history of Hanafi commercial law.

The sections on usury (riba) are among the most juristically significant in the recension. The Maliki and Hanafi schools held somewhat different positions on the precise definition of usurious transactions, on the classes of goods to which the prohibition applies, and on the circumstances under which certain transaction types become prohibited. Imam Muhammad's presentation of both positions with their reasoning is one of the earliest available systematic treatments of these important doctrinal differences.

The family law sections address the traditions on marriage, divorce, and related matters through the comparative lens that characterizes the recension throughout. The Maliki and Hanafi schools differed on several important questions of family law, including the permissibility of marriage without a guardian, the conditions governing certain types of divorce pronouncements, and the rulings on custody after divorce. Imam Muhammad's documentation of these differences at their earliest systematized stage is historically invaluable.

The inheritance sections present the Maliki traditions on the calculation of inheritance shares alongside the Hanafi positions. Islamic inheritance law is a highly technical domain in which small differences in legal principles can produce significantly different outcomes in specific cases, and Imam Muhammad's careful comparative presentation helps readers understand where and why the two schools' inheritance calculations diverge. The commercial and family law sections of the recension thus represent one of the earliest systematic comparative fiqh documents in Islamic legal history — a record of how the two dominant legal traditions of the early period understood the same questions and reasoned their way to different answers. For historians of Islamic law, this comparative dimension makes the recension irreplaceable as a primary source for the formative period of legal differentiation.
